title: Raft Consensus
text:
Raft is a consensus algorithm designed to be easier to understand than Paxos. In Raft, time is divided into terms, and each term begins with a leader election. A follower that hears no heartbeat within its randomized election timeout becomes a candidate, increments the term, and requests votes; a candidate that receives votes from a majority of the cluster becomes leader for that term.

Once elected, the Raft leader accepts client writes and replicates them as log entries to followers via AppendEntries RPCs. An entry is considered committed once the leader has replicated it on a majority of servers, at which point it is applied to the state machine. Raft guarantees that committed entries are never lost as long as a majority of servers survive.

title: Paxos Roles and Phases
text:
Classic Paxos defines three roles: proposers, acceptors, and learners. Proposers suggest values, acceptors vote on which value is chosen, and learners discover the chosen value. A single node may play multiple roles, and safety holds as long as a majority of acceptors is available.

Paxos reaches agreement in two phases. In the prepare phase, a proposer sends a proposal number n and acceptors promise not to accept proposals numbered lower than n. In the accept phase, the proposer asks acceptors to accept a value for proposal n, and the value is chosen once a majority of acceptors accept it.

title: BM25 Ranking Function
text:
BM25 is a lexical ranking function that scores a document for a query by combining term frequency, inverse document frequency, and document length normalization. Its two tunable parameters are k1, which controls how quickly term-frequency contribution saturates, and b, which controls how strongly scores are normalized by document length relative to the average.

Compared to plain TF-IDF, the key improvement in BM25 is term-frequency saturation: the tenth occurrence of a term adds far less score than the first, because the contribution asymptotically approaches a ceiling determined by k1. This prevents documents that simply repeat a query term many times from dominating the ranking.

title: HNSW Index Structure
text:
HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) is a graph-based approximate nearest neighbor index. It builds a multi-layer graph where upper layers contain a sparse subset of points with long-range links and the bottom layer contains all points with short-range links. A search starts at the top layer, greedily descends toward the query, and refines the result in the densest bottom layer.

The main HNSW search-time parameter is ef_search, the size of the dynamic candidate list kept during traversal. Raising ef_search increases recall because more candidate neighbors are explored, but it increases query latency roughly linearly, so it is the primary knob for trading accuracy against speed.

title: FAISS IVF Indexes
text:
An IVF (inverted file) index in FAISS partitions the vector space with a coarse quantizer, typically k-means with nlist centroids. Each database vector is assigned to its nearest centroid's inverted list, so at query time only a fraction of the database needs to be scanned.

At search time, the nprobe parameter controls how many inverted lists nearest to the query are scanned. A small nprobe is fast but can miss true neighbors that fell into unscanned cells, while a large nprobe approaches exhaustive search accuracy at proportionally higher latency.

title: Product Quantization
text:
Product quantization (PQ) compresses vectors by splitting each vector into m subvectors and quantizing each subvector separately against its own codebook of 256 centroids. Each subvector is then stored as a single byte, the index of its nearest centroid.

The memory savings of PQ are large: a 768-dimensional float32 vector occupies 3072 bytes, but with m equal to 96 subquantizers it compresses to 96 bytes, a 32x reduction. The cost is approximation error in distance computations, which is why PQ indexes are often paired with a reranking step that rescoress top candidates with exact distances.

title: Cross-Encoders vs Bi-Encoders
text:
A cross-encoder scores a query-document pair by concatenating the two texts and passing them jointly through a transformer, letting every query token attend to every document token. This joint attention makes cross-encoders substantially more accurate at relevance judgment than bi-encoders, which encode query and document into separate vectors and compare them with a similarity function.

The drawback of a cross-encoder is cost: it cannot precompute document representations, so every query-document pair requires a full forward pass. For this reason cross-encoders are used as rerankers over a small candidate set, typically the top 20 to 100 results from a cheaper first-stage retriever.

title: Redis Persistence
text:
Redis offers RDB persistence, which takes point-in-time snapshots of the dataset at configured intervals by forking the process and writing a compact binary file. RDB files are ideal for backups and fast restarts, but any writes made after the last snapshot are lost on a crash.

The alternative is AOF (append-only file) persistence, in which Redis logs every write command it receives. The AOF can be configured to fsync every second or on every command, bounding data loss to that window, and Redis rewrites the AOF in the background to keep it from growing without bound.

title: PostgreSQL MVCC
text:
PostgreSQL implements multi-version concurrency control (MVCC) by keeping multiple versions of a row in the table itself. Each row version carries xmin and xmax system columns recording the transaction IDs that created and deleted it, and each transaction's snapshot determines which versions are visible to it, so readers never block writers.

Because updates create new row versions rather than modifying in place, dead tuples accumulate as bloat. The VACUUM process reclaims space from dead tuples that no active snapshot can see, and autovacuum must keep up with update-heavy workloads or tables grow and scans slow down.

title: Kafka Log Fundamentals
text:
A Kafka topic is divided into partitions, and each partition is an ordered, append-only log. Every record in a partition is assigned a monotonically increasing offset, and ordering is guaranteed only within a single partition, not across the topic.

Consumers in Kafka are organized into consumer groups. Each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer within a group, which is how Kafka parallelizes consumption while preserving per-partition order; adding more consumers than partitions leaves the extras idle.

title: Consistent Hashing
text:
Consistent hashing maps both keys and nodes onto the same circular hash ring, assigning each key to the first node clockwise from it. Its defining property is that when a node joins or leaves, only the keys in the affected arc move, on average 1/n of all keys, rather than nearly all keys as with modulo hashing.

Plain consistent hashing can distribute load unevenly when there are few nodes. Virtual nodes fix this: each physical node is hashed to many positions on the ring, which smooths the distribution and lets heterogeneous machines take load proportional to their assigned number of virtual nodes.

title: Bloom Filters
text:
A Bloom filter is a space-efficient probabilistic structure for set membership. It can return false positives, claiming an element is present when it is not, but it never returns false negatives: if the filter says an element is absent, it is definitely absent.

A Bloom filter's false positive rate is determined by its bit array size m, the number of hash functions k, and the number of inserted elements n. A common rule of thumb is that roughly 10 bits per element with the optimal k of about 7 yields a false positive rate near 1 percent.

title: LSM Trees
text:
A log-structured merge tree (LSM tree) absorbs writes into an in-memory memtable, which is flushed to disk as an immutable sorted file called an SSTable when it fills. Because all disk writes are sequential, LSM trees achieve very high write throughput compared to update-in-place structures.

The price of LSM design is read and space amplification: a lookup may need to consult the memtable and several SSTables across levels. Background compaction merges SSTables to bound the number of files a read must check, trading ongoing write bandwidth for faster reads.

title: B-Trees
text:
A B-tree is a balanced search tree with high fanout: each node holds many keys and children, so the tree stays shallow and a lookup touches only a few pages. This makes B-trees the default index structure in relational databases, where each node maps to a disk page.

When a B-tree page fills, it splits into two pages and promotes a separator key to the parent, keeping the tree balanced. Because keys within and across pages are kept in sorted order, B-trees serve range scans efficiently by walking sibling leaf pages in order.

title: CAP and PACELC
text:
The CAP theorem states that a distributed system cannot simultaneously guarantee consistency, availability, and partition tolerance; during a network partition, a system must choose between serving possibly stale data (availability) and refusing requests to stay consistent. Since partitions are unavoidable in real networks, the practical choice is between C and A during partitions.

PACELC extends CAP by observing that even without partitions there is a tradeoff: if a partition occurs (P), choose availability or consistency (A or C); else (E), choose between lower latency and stronger consistency (L or C). PACELC explains why systems like DynamoDB favor latency while systems like Spanner favor consistency even in healthy networks.

title: Prometheus Model
text:
Prometheus uses a pull-based model: the server scrapes metrics over HTTP from instrumented targets at a configured interval, rather than having applications push measurements. Targets expose a /metrics endpoint with counters, gauges, histograms, and summaries in a plain-text format.

Prometheus histograms record observations in cumulative buckets, and quantiles such as p95 are estimated at query time with the histogram_quantile function over bucket rates. The estimate's accuracy depends on bucket boundaries, so latency buckets must be chosen to bracket the SLO being measured.

title: gRPC Fundamentals
text:
gRPC is an RPC framework that runs over HTTP/2 and serializes messages with Protocol Buffers by default. HTTP/2 multiplexing lets many concurrent RPCs share one TCP connection without head-of-line blocking at the request level, and binary protobuf encoding keeps messages compact and fast to parse.

gRPC supports four call types: unary, server streaming, client streaming, and bidirectional streaming. Streaming RPCs keep a single HTTP/2 stream open and exchange a sequence of messages, which suits use cases like live updates and incremental result delivery.

title: HTTP Caching
text:
HTTP caching freshness is governed primarily by the Cache-Control header. A response with max-age tells caches how many seconds it may be served without revalidation, while directives like no-store forbid caching entirely and private restricts storage to the browser cache.

When a cached response becomes stale, a client can revalidate it cheaply with a conditional request: it sends the stored ETag in an If-None-Match header, and if the resource is unchanged the server answers 304 Not Modified with no body, saving bandwidth while renewing freshness.

title: Token Bucket Algorithm
text:
The token bucket algorithm enforces a rate limit by adding tokens to a bucket at a fixed refill rate up to a maximum capacity; each request consumes a token and is rejected or delayed when the bucket is empty. Because the bucket can accumulate tokens while idle, token bucket permits short bursts up to the bucket capacity while holding the long-term average to the refill rate.

The leaky bucket algorithm, by contrast, drains requests at a constant output rate regardless of arrival pattern, smoothing traffic but forbidding bursts. Token bucket is the usual choice for API rate limiting because it tolerates bursty clients, while leaky bucket suits traffic shaping where a steady downstream rate matters.

title: Circuit Breaker Pattern
text:
A circuit breaker protects callers from a failing dependency by tracking recent failures and moving through three states: closed, open, and half-open. In the closed state requests flow normally while failures are counted; when the failure rate crosses a threshold the breaker trips to open, and all calls fail fast without touching the dependency.

After a cooldown period, the breaker enters half-open and lets a limited number of probe requests through. If the probes succeed the breaker resets to closed; if they fail it snaps back to open, so recovery is tested without subjecting the struggling dependency to full traffic.

title: Load Balancing Strategies
text:
Round-robin load balancing rotates requests across backends in order, which is simple and fair when requests are uniform. Least-connections instead routes each request to the backend with the fewest active connections, which adapts better when request durations vary widely, since slow requests naturally steer new traffic away from busy servers.

For cache servers, consistent-hash load balancing routes each key to the same backend every time, maximizing cache hit rates. This matters because random or round-robin assignment would spread the same hot keys across all cache nodes, multiplying memory use and lowering hit ratios.

title: Change Data Capture
text:
Log-based change data capture (CDC) reads a database's write-ahead log or binlog to produce an ordered stream of row-level changes without adding query load or triggers to the source database. Because it observes the same log the database uses for durability, log-based CDC captures every committed change exactly in commit order.

A common CDC pitfall is dual writes: an application that writes to the database and separately publishes an event can produce inconsistency when one write succeeds and the other fails. The outbox pattern fixes this by writing the event into an outbox table in the same transaction as the data change, then letting CDC relay the outbox to the message broker.

title: Database Sharding
text:
Hash sharding assigns each row to a shard by hashing its key, which spreads load evenly but destroys key ordering, making range queries scatter across all shards. Range sharding keeps contiguous key ranges together, serving range scans from few shards but risking hotspots when writes concentrate on one range, such as monotonically increasing IDs or timestamps.

Resharding, changing the number or boundaries of shards, is the operationally hardest part of sharding. Techniques include pre-splitting into many small logical shards that are moved between physical nodes, and directory-based sharding where a lookup service maps keys to shards so mappings can change without rehashing everything.

title: Write-Ahead Logging
text:
Write-ahead logging (WAL) is the durability rule that a change must be recorded in the log on stable storage before the corresponding data pages are modified. After a crash, the database replays the log to redo committed changes and undo uncommitted ones, restoring a consistent state.

The fsync at commit is the latency floor of durable transactions, since the log record must actually reach disk. Group commit amortizes this cost by batching several transactions' log records into one fsync, raising throughput dramatically under concurrency at the cost of a small added latency for the earliest transaction in each batch.

title: Quorum Replication
text:
In quorum-based replication with N replicas, writes go to W replicas and reads consult R replicas. Choosing R + W > N guarantees that every read quorum overlaps every write quorum in at least one replica, so a read always sees at least one copy of the most recent write.

Dynamo-style systems offer sloppy quorums: during failures, writes are accepted by healthy nodes outside the key's home set and carry hinted handoff markers. When the home replicas recover, the hints are handed back, restoring placement; this favors write availability at the cost of weakening the read-overlap guarantee of strict quorums.

title: Text Embeddings
text:
A text embedding model maps a passage into a dense fixed-dimension vector such that semantically similar texts land close together. Similarity is usually measured with cosine similarity, which compares vector direction and ignores magnitude, and many models normalize outputs so cosine similarity equals dot product.

Embedding dimensionality is a tradeoff: higher-dimensional vectors can encode finer distinctions but cost proportionally more memory and compute per comparison, since index size and distance computation scale linearly with dimension. Common sentence embedding models range from 384 to 1024 dimensions, with 384-dimensional models often chosen when index memory is constrained.

title: Measuring ANN Recall
text:
Recall@k for an approximate nearest neighbor index is the fraction of the true k nearest neighbors, as computed by exact brute-force search, that the index returns in its top k. It is measured by running the same query set through both exact and approximate search and comparing result sets.

Benchmarking an ANN index means tracing its recall-latency curve: sweep the search-time parameter, such as ef_search or nprobe, and record recall and query latency at each setting. Reporting a recall number without its latency point, or vice versa, is meaningless because either can be improved arbitrarily by sacrificing the other.

title: Two-Stage Retrieval
text:
A two-stage retrieval pipeline uses a fast first-stage retriever, such as BM25 or a bi-encoder ANN index, to produce a candidate set, then applies an expensive, accurate reranker, typically a cross-encoder, to reorder only those candidates. This structure gets most of the accuracy of scoring every document with the expensive model at a tiny fraction of the cost.

The candidate depth, how many first-stage results are reranked, is the pipeline's central tradeoff. A deeper candidate list raises the ceiling on final quality because the reranker can rescue relevant documents ranked low by the first stage, but reranking latency grows linearly with depth, so production systems commonly rerank between 20 and 100 candidates.

title: Distributed Rate Limiting
text:
Rate limiting across multiple API servers requires shared state, commonly counters or token buckets stored in Redis keyed by client identifier. Each server checks and updates the shared counter on every request, so the limit applies globally rather than per server instance.

A read-then-write implementation of a shared counter has a race condition: two servers can read the same count, both conclude the request is allowed, and both increment, exceeding the limit. The fix is to make the check-and-update atomic, either with a Redis Lua script that runs as a single operation or with atomic commands like INCR combined with EXPIRE.

title: Connection Pooling
text:
Opening a database connection is expensive: TCP setup, TLS negotiation, authentication, and backend process allocation. A connection pool keeps a set of connections open and lends them to requests, so steady-state request latency excludes connection setup and the database is protected from connection storms.

PgBouncer, the standard PostgreSQL pooler, offers session, transaction, and statement pooling modes. Transaction pooling gives the highest connection reuse because a server connection is only held for the duration of a transaction, but it breaks session-level features such as prepared statements and advisory locks that assume a stable backend connection.
